title: Reviewing Figurative Language and Communication Skills
simple_explanation: This page is like a treasure hunt for special words in poems! You're learning cool tricks that poets use to make their writing fun and colourful. You're also practicing how to tell your friends what you think of their poems and how to talk to people clearly to get things done.
detailed_explanation: You'll explore six important types: rhyme, alliteration, onomatopoeia, simile, metaphor, and personification. Knowing these will help you write better poems and understand them deeply. Another important skill is giving feedback on poems. You'll learn to look at your partner's poems, spot these language features, and explain what you like about their writing. The page also has a 'Global Perspectives Challenge' about how we communicate. It makes you think about all the different ways people talk to each other, both near and far, and how important good communication is for achieving your dreams. Finally, there's a checklist of all the amazing things you can now do, like reading poems aloud, discussing ideas, and using powerful words in your own writing.

examples_expanded.text: Rhyme: 'The cat sat on the mat.' (cat, mat)
examples_expanded.context: This example shows how we use the concept of exploring figurative language and communication in a real sentence.
This example shows how we use the concept of exploring figurative language and communication in a real sentence.

examples_expanded.text: Onomatopoeia: 'The bee went buzz!' (buzz sounds like what it is)
examples_expanded.context: This example shows how we use the concept of exploring figurative language and communication in a real sentence.

examples_expanded.text: Simile: 'Her smile was as bright as the sun.' (uses 'as' or 'like' to compare)
examples_expanded.context: This example shows how we use the concept of exploring figurative language and communication in a real sentence.

examples_expanded.text: Metaphor: 'The classroom was a zoo.' (says one thing IS another to show comparison, no 'as' or 'like')
examples_expanded.context: This example shows how we use the concept of exploring figurative language and communication in a real sentence.

examples_expanded.text: Personification: 'The wind whispered secrets through the trees.' (giving human qualities to non-human things)
examples_expanded.context: This example shows how we use the concept of exploring figurative language and communication in a real sentence.

guided_practice: Identify the meaning of 'rhyme' in a sentence. Remember it means: Words that have the same ending sound.
Match the word 'alliteration' with a similar concept from your own experience.
Look at the first example again. How would you modify it to make it your own?
application_tasks: Create 3 of your own sentences using the core idea of Exploring Figurative Language and Communication.
Imagine you are teaching this topic to a friend. Write down exactly what you would say.
challenge_tasks: Combine the concept of 'rhyme' with something you learned in a previous chapter to write a short paragraph.
Can you find an exception to this rule or a creative way to bend it? Explain how.
key_points: Figurative language makes poetry vivid and engaging.
You can identify and explain different types of figurative language.
Giving constructive feedback on poems helps others improve.
Effective communication is key to achieving personal and community goals.

vocabulary.definition: Words that have the same ending sound.
When words that are close together start with the same sound.
A word that sounds like the noise it describes.
Compares two different things using 'like' or 'as'.
Compares two different things by saying one thing IS the other.
Giving human qualities or actions to objects or animals.
To carefully judge or assess something.
To share information, ideas, or feelings with others.
Showing feeling or emotion when you speak or read.
The exact, real meaning of words.
A meaning that is not exact or real, like in figurative language.
Words that sound the same but have different spellings and meanings (e.g., 'to', 'too', 'two').
A word that names a person, place, thing, or idea.
A word that describes a noun.
